inline_review:
commit ab3f02e0e8285802214ac15514f37de4137804bb
Author: Hao Ge <hao.ge@linux.dev>

mm/alloc_tag: clear codetag for pages allocated before page_ext initialization

This commit introduces tracking for pages allocated before page_ext
initialization to prevent a warning when they are later freed. It uses a
fixed-size array of 8192 PFNs to track early allocations and sets their
codetags to CODETAG_EMPTY once page_ext is available.

> diff --git a/lib/alloc_tag.c b/lib/alloc_tag.c
> index 58991ab09d84..ed1bdcf1f8ab 100644
> --- a/lib/alloc_tag.c
> +++ b/lib/alloc_tag.c

[ ... ]

> +#define EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX		8192
> +
> +static unsigned long early_pfns[EARLY_ALLOC_PFN_MAX] __initdata;

On large systems with many CPUs, could the number of early buddy allocations
(e.g., for per-CPU scheduler data, slab caches, and trace ring buffers)
exceed 8192?

If the array overflows, the code stops tracking further allocations, which
would cause the WARN_ONCE to trigger when those untracked pages are freed.

[ ... ]

> +static void __init clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs(void)
> +{
> +	unsigned int i;
> +
> +	if (static_key_enabled(&mem_profiling_compressed))
> +		return;
> +
> +	rcu_assign_pointer(alloc_tag_add_early_pfn_ptr, NULL);
> +	/* Make sure we are not racing with __alloc_tag_add_early_pfn() */
> +	synchronize_rcu();
> +
> +	for (i = 0; i < atomic_read(&early_pfn_count); i++) {

Could there be a race window during page_ext initialization?

In page_ext_init(), memory sections are initialized sequentially. As soon
as a section's page_ext is allocated, get_page_tag_ref() will return true
for pages within that section.

However, the early allocations are not explicitly set to CODETAG_EMPTY until
clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs() executes at the end of the initialization
process.

If an early-allocated page is freed during this window (for example, while
cond_resched() allows other threads to run), __pgalloc_tag_sub() will
fetch the tag reference and see a NULL ref.ct because the tag hasn't been
cleared to CODETAG_EMPTY yet, erroneously triggering the WARN_ONCE.

> diff --git a/mm/page_alloc.c b/mm/page_alloc.c
> index 111b54df8a3c..b1c5430cad4e 100644
> --- a/mm/page_alloc.c
> +++ b/mm/page_alloc.c

[ ... ]

> @@ -1252,10 +1252,18 @@ void __pgalloc_tag_add(struct page *page, struct task_struct *task,
>  	union pgtag_ref_handle handle;
>  	union codetag_ref ref;
>  
> -	if (get_page_tag_ref(page, &ref, &handle)) {
> +	if (likely(get_page_tag_ref(page, &ref, &handle))) {
>  		alloc_tag_add(&ref, task->alloc_tag, PAGE_SIZE * nr);
>  		update_page_tag_ref(handle, &ref);
>  		put_page_tag_ref(handle);
> +	} else {
> +		/*
> +		 * page_ext is not available yet, record the pfn so we can
> +		 * clear the tag ref later when page_ext is initialized.
> +		 */
> +		alloc_tag_add_early_pfn(page_to_pfn(page));

Does this code lose track of tail pages for high-order allocations?

The __pgalloc_tag_add() function receives an allocation size in the nr
parameter, but alloc_tag_add_early_pfn() only records the head page's PFN.

When clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs() runs, it will only apply CODETAG_EMPTY
to the head page. If this early high-order allocation is subsequently split
(e.g., via split_page()), pgalloc_tag_split() will attempt to propagate the
tag:

lib/alloc_tag.c:pgalloc_tag_split() {
    ...
    tag = __pgalloc_tag_get(&folio->page);
    if (!tag)
        return;
    ...
}

Because __pgalloc_tag_get() evaluates !is_codetag_empty() and returns NULL
for CODETAG_EMPTY, the tail pages never receive the CODETAG_EMPTY tag. When
these tail pages are eventually freed individually, __pgalloc_tag_sub()
will fetch their uninitialized tag references and trigger the WARN_ONCE
this patch is meant to fix.
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Incomplete tag initialization for early high-order allocations (Tail pages missing)","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"When an early high-order allocation occurs, `__pgalloc_tag_add` records only the head page's PFN in `early_pfns` and discards the allocation size (`nr`). Later, `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs` applies `CODETAG_EMPTY` exclusively to the head page. The tail pages remain uninitialized (effectively `NULL` since `page_ext` is zeroed). If this early high-order allocation is subsequently split (e.g., via `split_page()`), `pgalloc_tag_split` will attempt to propagate the tag but immediately return because `__pgalloc_tag_get` evaluates `!is_codetag_empty(&ref)` and returns `NULL` for `CODETAG_EMPTY`. As a result, the tail pages never receive the `CODETAG_EMPTY` tag. When these tail pages are eventually freed individually, `__pgalloc_tag_sub` will fetch their uninitialized tag references and trigger the exact `WARN_ONCE(ref && !ref->ct, "alloc_tag was not set\n")` warning this patch was designed to fix. The tracking must include the order/size of the allocation and propagate the empty tag to all pages in the allocation."},{"problem":"Race window during page_ext initialization causes false positive warnings","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"In `page_ext_init()`, memory sections are initialized sequentially. As soon as a section's `page_ext` is allocated, `get_page_tag_ref()` will return `true` for pages within that section. However, the codetag references for early allocations are not explicitly set to `CODETAG_EMPTY` until `clear_early_alloc_pfn_tag_refs()` executes at the very end of the initialization process (via `invoke_init_callbacks`). Because `cond_resched()` is called during the section initialization loop, other threads or interrupt handlers can execute. If an early-allocated page is freed during this window, `__pgalloc_tag_sub()` will fetch the tag reference (which now successfully resolves) and check `ref.ct`. Since the tag has not yet been cleared to `CODETAG_EMPTY`, it will evaluate to `NULL` and erroneously trigger the `WARN_ONCE`. This completely defeats the purpose of the patch for pages freed during the initialization window."},{"problem":"Hardcoded 8192 array size will overflow on large systems and trigger the bug","severity":"Medium","severity_explanation":"The patch uses a fixed-size `early_pfns` array limited to 8192 entries to track early allocations. On large systems (such as high-end NUMA servers with thousands of CPUs), the number of buddy allocations performed during early boot (e.g., for per-CPU scheduler data, slab caches, and trace ring buffers) can easily exceed 8192. When the array overflows, the code simply prints a warning and silently stops tracking further early allocations. When these untracked early pages are subsequently freed, their codetag references will be `NULL`, triggering the `WARN_ONCE(ref && !ref->ct)` inside `alloc_tag_sub_check()`. A dynamic allocation mechanism or fallback strategy is required to correctly handle systems with large memory and core counts."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit ab3f02e0e8285802214ac15514f37de4137804bb

> +	synchronize_rcu();
